note: Gap signals are thematic clusters derived from DCWF STRM analysis. DCWF is unique among Tier 1 frameworks: its 99.7% match rate means gap signals emerge almost entirely from weak-strength patterns (≤3) rather than no-relationship boundaries. 172 very-weak elements (strength ≤2) were analyzed thematically. The majority are cybersecurity/intelligence/forensic-specific with legitimate low scores. Four gap signals identified — three corroborate existing gaps from prior STRMs, one is new. Per ADR-014, gaps are registered here and resolved during Phase 1D Synthesis.

gaps.title: Data/AI Incident Response and Recovery

triggering_fdes.name: Confirm what is known about an intrusion and discover new information
Report intelligence-derived significant network events and intrusions
Perform event correlation using information gathered from a variety of sources

gaps.nearest_miss: RM-K-001 (risk quantification frameworks), OP-K-007 (IT service management principles)
gaps.description: DCWF defines incident response coordination, intrusion analysis, event correlation, and incident reporting competencies across multiple work roles. WIDAI covers risk management and operational monitoring but lacks structured incident response competencies specific to data/AI failures — data breach response coordination, AI model failure escalation, data pipeline disaster recovery, and model rollback procedures. The weak-strength mappings (all scoring 2) confirm that DCWF IR competencies find only marginal overlap with WIDAI's risk and operations KSAs.
gaps.cross_strm_evidence: Second framework (after NICE) to independently surface data/AI incident response as a WIDAI gap. NICE surfaced it via cybersecurity IR competencies; DCWF surfaces it via federal incident coordination requirements. The consistent weak-strength pattern across both frameworks strengthens the case for dedicated data/AI IR KSAs in WIDAI.
gaps.authoring_guidance: See NICE-GAP-002 authoring guidance. The DCWF corroboration strengthens the case for inclusion. Consider both Knowledge and Skill KSAs covering data breach notification, AI model failure escalation, data pipeline disaster recovery, model rollback protocols, and cross-functional IR coordination.
gaps.synthesis_recommendation: Upgraded to high priority based on dual-framework corroboration (NICE + DCWF). Both federal cybersecurity frameworks independently identify IR as a boundary-zone gap. Data/AI IR is distinct from cybersecurity IR in root causes, stakeholders, and remediation paths.

gaps.title: Workforce Planning, Budgeting, and Organizational Design for Data/AI Functions

triggering_fdes.name: Lead and oversee budget, staffing, and contracting
Maintain awareness of internal and external cyber organization structures, strengths, and employment
Ability to ensure security practices are followed throughout the acquisition process
Establish and oversee waiver processes for cyber career field entry and training qualifications

gaps.description: DCWF defines workforce management competencies — budgeting, staffing, contracting, organizational structure awareness, acquisition oversight, and career field qualification management — that map weakly to WIDAI. WIDAI covers strategic planning and talent management at a high level but lacks operational workforce planning competencies: budget management for data/AI programs, staffing models, vendor/contractor management, organizational design for data/AI functions, and career pathway development. These are particularly relevant for CDAIO roles building or restructuring data/AI organizations.
gaps.corroborates: ONET-GAP-004 (Project and Time Management) — partial. Both gaps surface operational management competencies, though DCWF-GAP-002 focuses specifically on workforce/organizational management rather than project management.
gaps.cross_strm_evidence: O*NET surfaced project/time management gaps; DCWF surfaces workforce planning and organizational design gaps. Together they indicate WIDAI's LS domain covers strategic leadership well but may underserve operational management competencies that CDOs/CDAIOs exercise daily.
gaps.authoring_guidance: Consider KSAs such as: 'Knowledge of workforce planning methodologies for data and AI functions, including staffing models, budget management, vendor/contractor strategy, and organizational design patterns' (Knowledge, LS domain) and 'Skill in designing and managing data/AI organizational structures, including career pathways, role definitions, competency frameworks, and succession planning' (Skill, LS domain).
gaps.synthesis_recommendation: Moderate priority. Evaluate against DDAT STRM (which defines digital/data team management competencies) and SFIA (which defines IT workforce management skills). If corroborated, this gap directly serves the CDAIO operating model use case.

gaps.title: Supply Chain Integrity for Data and AI

triggering_fdes.name: Ability to ensure security practices are followed throughout the acquisition process
Maintain awareness of internal and external cyber organization structures, strengths, and employment

gaps.nearest_miss: RM-K-003 (third-party risk assessment), AG-K-008 (AI supply chain concepts)
gaps.description: DCWF defines acquisition lifecycle competencies including security practices in procurement and vendor/contractor oversight. WIDAI covers third-party risk assessment and emerging AI supply chain concepts but lacks dedicated supply chain integrity competencies for data/AI — training data provenance verification, model supply chain validation, data vendor quality assurance, and AI component bill-of-materials management.
gaps.corroborates: NICE-GAP-005 (Supply Chain Integrity for Data and AI)
gaps.cross_strm_evidence: Second framework (after NICE) to surface supply chain integrity as a boundary-zone gap. NICE framed it via cybersecurity supply chain security; DCWF frames it via federal acquisition lifecycle requirements. The pattern is consistent: both frameworks define supply chain competencies that translate to data/AI contexts but find no dedicated WIDAI KSAs.
gaps.authoring_guidance: See NICE-GAP-005 authoring guidance. DCWF corroboration adds federal acquisition lifecycle perspective. Consider whether EU AI Act STRM (supply chain documentation requirements) provides the third corroboration needed to upgrade priority.
gaps.synthesis_recommendation: Low priority as standalone, but accumulating cross-framework evidence. Two frameworks now surface this gap. If EU AI Act or NIST AI RMF STRMs corroborate, upgrade to moderate.

gaps.title: Test and Evaluation Methodology for Data/AI Systems

triggering_fdes.name: Determine level of assurance of developed capabilities based on test results
Report test and evaluation deficiencies and possible solutions to appropriate personnel
Assess the technical health of the cyberspace operator work role

gaps.nearest_miss: AI-S-015 (model validation techniques), DQ-S-003 (data quality testing)
gaps.description: DCWF defines test and evaluation (T&E) competencies for determining assurance levels, reporting deficiencies, and assessing capability health. WIDAI covers model validation and data quality testing but lacks a structured T&E methodology competency — the discipline of designing test plans, executing evaluation protocols, determining assurance levels, and reporting results for data/AI systems. This is distinct from model validation (which focuses on statistical performance) — T&E encompasses operational readiness, integration testing, user acceptance, and conformity assessment. Particularly relevant given DCWF Work Role 672 (AI Test and Evaluation Specialist) maps to WIDAI.
gaps.cross_strm_evidence: First framework to surface T&E as a distinct gap. O*NET and NICE did not surface this because their T&E elements are cybersecurity-specific. DCWF's AI T&E Specialist work role creates a direct connection to data/AI T&E competencies.
gaps.authoring_guidance: Consider a Skill: 'Skill in designing and executing test and evaluation plans for data and AI systems, including operational readiness assessment, integration testing, conformity assessment against standards, and structured deficiency reporting with remediation tracking.' This would serve AI T&E Specialist, AI Risk and Ethics Specialist, and senior governance roles. Evaluate whether this is distinct enough from model validation (AI-S-015) to warrant a separate KSA.
gaps.synthesis_recommendation: Low priority as standalone. Evaluate against EU AI Act STRM (which requires conformity assessment capabilities for high-risk AI systems) and NIST AI RMF STRM (which defines testing expectations in the MAP and MEASURE functions). If regulatory frameworks confirm T&E as a distinct workforce competency beyond model validation, upgrade to moderate.
